tldr: Moving from Stripe (PSP, you are the merchant) to Paddle (Merchant of Record) shifts tax, invoicing and seller-of-record liability to Paddle in exchange for higher per-transaction fees and less checkout flexibility. Plan 2-4 weeks; expect customer/subscription re-creation, not a 1:1 token import.
why_switch: Offload global sales tax / VAT / GST collection and remittance to Paddle
Stop tracking tax nexus thresholds across US states and EU countries
Built-in invoicing, dunning and revenue recovery tuned for SaaS subscriptions
Reduce compliance scope — Paddle is seller of record, you sell to Paddle
Simpler chargeback handling (Paddle fronts the dispute process)
One vendor for payments + tax + invoicing instead of Stripe + Stripe Tax + accounting glue
Fewer accounting integrations needed at small/mid scale
what_to_export: Customer list (email, name, billing address, country) via Stripe Dashboard CSV or list API
Active subscriptions with plan, interval, current_period_end, quantity
Products and prices catalog (name, currency, amount, interval)
Coupons / promo codes still in use
Tax IDs collected from B2B customers
Invoice and payment history for accounting / audit (read-only archive)
Webhook event log or last known state per subscription, for reconciliation
Card / payment method tokens — only via Stripe's PCI Data Export to a PCI-compliant target; most teams skip this and re-collect
Refund and dispute history (reference only, not portable)
Metadata fields you rely on (Stripe customer / subscription metadata)
Connect account data — N/A on Paddle, document separately if used
export_method: Use Stripe Dashboard CSV exports for customers, subscriptions, products and invoices. For programmatic export, use Stripe's list endpoints for customers, subscriptions, prices and products. Card-on-file export requires a formal PCI Data Export request to Stripe and a PCI-DSS Level 1 receiving environment — usually not worth it for a Paddle move; plan to re-collect payment methods instead. Keep at least 24 months of invoice history for accounting and disputes.
import_method: Re-create products and prices in Paddle (Dashboard or API). Import customers as Paddle customers. Subscriptions are typically NOT bulk-imported with card-on-file; the common pattern is to email existing customers, route them to a Paddle checkout to re-authorize, then cancel the Stripe subscription on first successful Paddle charge. For larger books, ask Paddle support about an assisted migration. Run both stacks in parallel during cutover and reconcile daily.

concept_mapping.target: Paddle Transaction / Invoice (issued by Paddle as MoR)

concept_mapping.note: Fundamentally different: Paddle is seller of record, not a tax calculator.
Different event names and payload shape — every consumer needs review.

concept_mapping.target: Paddle customer portal / hosted management links

concept_mapping.note: Paddle is single-seller; keep Stripe Connect or pick another rail.

concept_mapping.target: Built into Paddle's risk engine, not separately configurable

effort: 2-4 weeks for a small-to-mid SaaS (under ~5k active subs). Larger books, complex tax footprints or marketplace-style setups: 4-8+ weeks, or not viable if you depend on Stripe Connect.
gotchas: Paddle is Merchant of Record — Paddle is the seller on the customer's card statement and on the invoice; revenue recognition and accounting flow change (you book revenue from Paddle, not from end customer).

Tax handling differs fundamentally: with Stripe Tax you still file and remit; with Paddle, Paddle files and remits VAT / GST / US sales tax on your behalf, but historical Stripe-period filings stay your responsibility.
Saved cards usually cannot be moved 1:1 — most teams re-collect payment methods, which causes some involuntary churn during the cutover window.
Higher effective fees: Paddle's blended rate (~5% + $0.50) is materially above Stripe's ~2.9% + $0.30 because tax + MoR services are bundled in.
Marketplaces, split payouts and Connect-style flows are not a fit — Paddle is built for single-seller SaaS.
Webhook event names and payloads are different; any logic keyed on Stripe events (invoice.payment_succeeded, etc.) must be rewritten against Paddle's event model.
Less checkout customization than Stripe Elements — branded checkout is more constrained.
Refunds, chargebacks and billing emails come from Paddle's domain, which can confuse customers; update billing FAQ and support macros before cutover.

email.subject: Quick update: we're moving billing to Paddle
email.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

We're moving our billing from Stripe to Paddle to simplify global tax handling. From now on, your invoices will be issued by Paddle as the seller of record, and you may see "Paddle" on your card statement instead of our company name.

What you need to do: the next time you're charged, we'll ask you to confirm your payment method on a secure Paddle checkout page. Your subscription, plan and renewal date stay the same.

Nothing else changes — same product, same price, same team. If you have any questions about an invoice or payment method, just reply to this email.

Thanks for being a customer.

— The {{company}} team
